sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation
sources.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting suspected-drug identity, indication, dose-count or exposure duration, route, reaction description, severity, timing, examination, and structured allergy record. It does not diagnose drug allergy, stop a medicine, generate treatment, or make a referral.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.1.1 and Tables 1–3 — assessment, reaction patterns, and timing
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting history, examination, timing after exposure, erythema, urticaria, angioedema, respiratory or cardiovascular features, macules, papules, fixed eruption, fever, lymphadenopathy, mucosal erosion, vesicles, blistering, detachment, pustules, photosensitivity, and vasculitic features without asserting a diagnosis.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.9 — recording and sharing drug-allergy information
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports structured recording of drug name, reaction signs, symptoms, severity, date and time, indication, number of doses or days before onset, route, status, and specialist-investigation results already available.

exact_relevant_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting history, examination, timing after exposure, erythema, urticaria, angioedema, respiratory or cardiovascular features, macules, papules, fixed eruption, fever, lymphadenopathy, mucosal erosion, vesicles, blistering, detachment, pustules, photosensitivity, and vasculitic features without asserting a diagnosis.

sources.exact_document_title: Emergency treatment of anaphylaxis: Guidelines for healthcare providers
sources.population: Adults and children with suspected anaphylaxis.
sources.applicability_note: Used only to support anaphylaxis warning documentation associated with rash or urticaria; it does not convert isolated skin findings into anaphylaxis.

exact_sections.heading: Key recommendations; 2.1 Definition of anaphylaxis
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Recognises rapid symptoms with airway, breathing, or circulation problems and usually skin or mucosal changes such as urticaria or angioedema; skin changes alone are not anaphylaxis.

exact_sections.heading: Key recommendations and trigger discussion
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting known allergen exposure, food or drug triggers, action-plan discussion, and specialist follow-up after suspected anaphylaxis.
